column1: https://doi.org/10.15454/UDQLGE‡https://doi.org/10.15454/UDQLGE‡https://doi.org/10.15454/UDQLGE‡inrae://10.15454/UDQLGE‡‡doi:10.15454/UDQLGE‡2020-04-10T13:10:59Z‡2020-04-10T13:10:00Z‡‡"Replication Data for ""Large-scale multivariate dataset on the characterization of microbiota diversity
column3: metabolic spoilage volatilome and sensorial profiles of two industrially produced meat products subjected to changes in lactate concentration and packaging atmosphere"""‡Loux
column5:
Simon‡"Lien vers les données
Data provide detailed information on the diversity of bacterial communities present on 576 samples of raw pork or poultry sausages produced industrially in 2017. Bacterial growth dynamics and diversity were monitored throughout the refrigerated storage period to estimate the impact of packaging atmosphere and the use of potassium lactate as chemical preservative. The data include several types of analysis aiming at providing a comprehensive microbial ecology of spoilage during storage and how the process parameters do influence this phenomenon. The analysis includes: the gas content in packaging
column8: plate counts (total mesophilic aerobic flora and lactic acid bacteria)
column10:
meta-metabolomic quantification of volatile organic compounds and bacterial community metagenetic analysis. Bacterial diversity was monitored using two types of amplicon sequencing (16S rRNA and GyrB encoding genes) at different time points for the different conditions (576 samples for gyrB and 436 samples for 16S rDNA). Sequencing data were generated by using Illumina MiSeq. The sequencing data have been deposited in the bioproject PRJNA522361"‡Food Microbiota; Food and food processing; Meat Spoilage; Metabolomic; Metagenetic; Microbial Ecology; Microorganisms; Omics; UBO‡Dataset‡"RDL_TableS01_Sample_Data_V2.tab

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/J8ERW1‡https://doi.org/10.57745/J8ERW1‡https://doi.org/10.57745/J8ERW1‡espace-generique://10.57745/J8ERW1‡en‡doi:10.57745/J8ERW1‡2024-02-07T09:37:43Z‡2024-02-05T13:30:11Z‡‡Genetic diversity and population structure of Botryosphaeria dothidea and Neofusicoccum parvum on English walnut (Juglans regia L.) in France‡Pensec
column3:
Marie‡"Lien vers les données
Although widespread in nut orchards abroad and in other French crops such as vineyards
column4: dieback disease has been recently reported in France on walnut trees
column5: causing severe yield losses since 2015 onwards. The emergent nature of this disease encouraged us to explore its sources and pathways
column6: based on a population genetic study. This dataset contains genotypes of B. dothidea and N. parvum strains collected from French walnut trees (both symptomatic and asymptomatic walnut husks and twigs; 381 strains)
column8: Spanish walnut trees and Californian walnut and almond trees (24 strains). Genotypes were obtained the microsatellite marker sequencing technology developed and implemented at the Genome Transcriptome Facility of Bordeaux. A total of 11 loci were used for B. dothidea
column9: and 27 loci for N. parvum. The dataset also contains metadata (e.g. collection date
column10: origin) on each strain.  For each species
column11:
the genotype tables are constructed as follow: the name of the strains (1st column); the name of the loci identified as PROJECT-SPECIES-NUMBER OF THE LOCI (1st line); and the allele for each locus coded using a three-digit number. A different code represents a different allele in terms of microsatellite repeats. An allele coded with ""0"" corresponds to a missing data."‡Botryosphaeria; Genetic diversity; Juglans regia; Microsatellite; Neofusicoccum parvum; UBO‡Dataset‡"B-dothidea_metadata.tab

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/WRDJ3N‡https://doi.org/10.57745/WRDJ3N‡https://doi.org/10.57745/WRDJ3N‡espace-generique://10.57745/WRDJ3N‡en‡doi:10.57745/WRDJ3N‡2025-11-04T12:51:26Z‡2025-10-31T11:24:47Z‡‡Participatory and multi-disciplinary science dataset and surveys for the assessment of the microbiological and behavioural factors influencing fresh fruits and vegetables' waste at home.‡Marchal
column3:
Camille‡"Lien vers les données
Adopted in September 2015 by the EU
column4: the Sustainable Development Goal “12.3” targets to halve the per capita food waste at the retail and consumer level by 2030. Households account for 53% of food waste in Europe
column5: more than any other actor in the food chain. Fresh fruit and vegetables (FFV) are known to constitute the higher volumes of wasted food mainly associated with microbial contamination and spoilage. The FOODREST project was financed by the French National Research Agency (ANR-20-CE21-0006). Its goal was to better understand the microbiological and behavioral aspects of FFV waste at the household level
column6: and to propose efficient recommendations acceptable by households to reduce FFV waste. The FOODREST project aimed at i) studying microbiota associated with FFV storage facilities at home as well as identifying the predominant microorganisms and factors responsible for FFV spoilage
column7: ii) studying consumer perceptions and practices towards spoiled products and food waste
column8:
and iii) producing a set of efficient recommendations appropriate to consumer practices by evaluating the efficacy and acceptability of hygiene recommendations aiming at reducing food waste while guaranteeing food safety and by providing a best practices handbook and risk model for better food waste management at the consumer level."‡Consumer behavior; Food science; Food waste; Fruit; Microbiology; Storage; UBO; Vegetables; participatory science‡Dataset; Text; Other‡"Doc1a_Interview_Guide_1.docx

column2:
Roddy Michel‡"Lien vers les données

This is a dataset of 454 vernacular place names (toponyms) of fishing sites in the Bay of Toliara
column3: Southwest Madagascar. The data was collected as part of three fish landing surveys of the coral reef fishery conducted in i) 1989-1990 in six fishing villages (Besakoa
column9: ii) in 2009 in five fishing villages (Ambohitsabo
column14: and iii) in 2018-2019 in eight fishing villages (Ambohitsabo
column23: 904 fishing trips were monitored in 2009 and 2018-2019
column24:
respectively. The name of the fishing site(s) visited during each fishing trip was recorded by the fishers on a logsheet in the Vezo language. The Vezo is one of the ethnic groups in Southwest Madagascar whose identity relies on fishing practice.

The literal meaning of the place names is given in Malagasy
column26: and English as recorded during focus group discussion in 2022 in four out of the eight villages (Ambohitsabo
column31:
and/or 2018-2019) when each name was recorded was also indicated in the dataset.
